content.text: Why NFRs Are Different for Claude Integrations

Non-functional requirements (NFRs) for Claude integrations share some characteristics with NFRs for conventional software — availability, security, scalability — but have several dimensions that are unique to generative AI systems. Accuracy, hallucination rate, and context window utilisation have no direct equivalent in traditional software NFRs. The cost model (charged per token rather than per compute-hour) changes how scalability is specified. And the non-deterministic nature of LLM outputs changes what testability means.

NFRs that are written for conventional software and applied to Claude integrations without adaptation produce requirements that are either unmeasurable ('outputs must be accurate') or misspecified ('system must respond in under 1 second' when streaming makes the 1-second constraint technically impossible and the wrong metric anyway). This section covers the six NFR categories that apply to Claude integrations and how to specify them correctly.
The Six NFR Categories

attrs.alt: NFR taxonomy for Claude integrations: performance, reliability, accuracy, security, scalability, and observability

content.text: Every Claude integration requires NFRs across all six categories. The categories are not independent: a strict latency NFR drives architectural decisions (synchronous vs. streaming vs. async) that in turn affect reliability and scalability design. Specify all six before the integration architecture is finalised.
Performance NFRs
Performance NFRs for Claude integrations are more nuanced than for conventional APIs because the time-to-complete depends on output length, which is variable and partially under the system's control.

attrs.alt: Latency requirement selection by interaction pattern: synchronous short output, synchronous long output, and asynchronous/batch

content.text: Specifying Latency Requirements

Latency requirements must specify three things: the metric (wall-clock time to what event?), the percentile (P50 median is not useful for user experience; P95 is the minimum useful percentile), and the interaction pattern (synchronous non-streaming, synchronous streaming, or asynchronous).
Interaction Pattern
Latency Metric
Typical Target
When to Use
Synchronous non-streaming
Time from request to complete response received
P95 < 3 seconds
Short outputs (<200 words); task completion confirmations; classification results
Synchronous streaming
Time to first token (TTFT) + time to full response
TTFT P95 < 1s; full response P95 < 8s
Long responses; conversational interfaces; document drafting
Asynchronous / batch
Time from job submission to result available
P95 < 30 minutes
Document processing; report generation; large-volume batch work

A common NFR mistake is specifying 'response time < X seconds' without specifying the percentile. A mean response time of 2 seconds can coexist with a P95 of 12 seconds — which means 1 in 20 users waits 12 seconds. User experience is defined by the tail, not the mean. Always specify P95 at minimum; P99 for user-facing production systems.
Context Window Utilisation Limit

Context window utilisation affects both cost and latency. A prompt that uses 80% of the context window on a long conversation history will produce slower and more expensive responses than one that manages context efficiently. The NFR should specify a maximum context window utilisation per request:

# Context utilisation NFR enforcement check
# Run before each API call in production

import anthropic

def check_context_budget(
    messages: list[dict],
    system_prompt: str,
    model: str = "claude-opus-4-8",
    max_utilisation: float = 0.75,  # NFR: never exceed 75% of context
) -> dict:
    client = anthropic.Anthropic()

    # Count tokens without making the actual API call
    token_count = client.messages.count_tokens(
        model=model,
        system=system_prompt,
        messages=messages,
    )
    used = token_count.input_tokens

    # Model context limits (tokens)
    limits = {
        "claude-opus-4-8": 200_000,
        "claude-sonnet-4-6": 200_000,
        "claude-haiku-4-5-20251001": 200_000,
    }
    limit = limits.get(model, 200_000)
    utilisation = used / limit

    return {
        "tokens_used": used,
        "context_limit": limit,
        "utilisation": round(utilisation, 3),
        "within_budget": utilisation <= max_utilisation,
        "headroom_tokens": int(limit * max_utilisation) - used,
    }

# Example: check before sending
result = check_context_budget(
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "Summarise our last 20 exchanges."}],
    system_prompt="You are an HR policy assistant...",
)
if not result["within_budget"]:
    # Trim conversation history before calling API
    print(f"Context limit exceeded: {result['utilisation']:.1%} used")
else:
    print(f"OK: {result['utilisation']:.1%} used, {result['headroom_tokens']} tokens headroom")
Reliability NFRs

Reliability NFRs for Claude integrations must account for the fact that the Claude API is an external dependency with its own availability characteristics. An integration that assumes 100% API availability will fail when the API returns 429 (rate limit) or 529 (overloaded). The reliability NFR must specify both the target uptime and the fallback behaviour.
Availability Target
Express the availability target as a percentage and as a maximum acceptable downtime per period:
Availability Target
Maximum Downtime Per Month
Appropriate Use Case
7.3 hours
Internal productivity tools where downtime is inconvenient but not business-critical
3.6 hours
Department-level tools supporting daily workflows; downtime causes significant disruption
44 minutes
Business-critical tools; downtime directly affects customer SLAs or revenue
< 22 minutes
Mission-critical systems; requires multi-region or fallback architecture

Match the availability target to the business impact of downtime, not to an aspirational standard. An HR policy assistant does not need 99.9% availability; a customer-facing claims processing system might. Over-engineering availability targets increases cost and complexity without proportionate benefit.
Fallback Behaviour Specification
For every availability target below 99.9%, specify the fallback behaviour when the Claude API is unavailable:
Graceful degradation:
The feature continues to function without Claude but with reduced capability. Example: the search feature returns keyword results without AI-generated summaries.
Queue and retry:
Requests are queued and processed when the API recovers. Suitable for asynchronous use cases. The NFR must specify the maximum queue size and the queue timeout.
User notification and manual fallback:
The system informs the user that the AI feature is temporarily unavailable and provides instructions for the manual alternative. Suitable for non-time-critical use cases.
Circuit breaker:

After a defined number of consecutive failures, the integration stops attempting API calls for a defined period, preventing cascading failures. The NFR must specify the failure threshold and the circuit-open duration.
Accuracy NFRs

Accuracy NFRs are the most contentious category in Claude integrations because accuracy is contextual, multidimensional, and difficult to measure at scale. 'The system must be accurate' is not an NFR; 'The retrieval precision at K=5 must be ≥ 0.80 for in-scope queries, measured monthly on a fixed 100-query evaluation set' is.
Defining Accuracy Dimensions
Accuracy Dimension
Definition
How to Measure
Typical Target
Factual accuracy
Outputs are factually correct with respect to the knowledge base
Human review of sampled outputs against source documents
>= 95% correct for policy-critical domains
Retrieval precision (RAG)
Retrieved chunks are relevant to the query
Precision@K on fixed evaluation set
Hallucination rate
Claude does not generate information not in the knowledge base
Human review: count outputs containing ungrounded claims
< 2% of responses contain ungrounded claims
Appropriate referral rate
Out-of-scope queries are correctly declined and redirected
Sample 50 out-of-scope queries monthly; count correct referrals
>= 98% correctly redirected
Format compliance
Outputs conform to the specified format (length, structure, citation)
Automated check on sampled outputs against format rules
>= 99% format-compliant

Not every accuracy dimension applies to every use case. A document classification integration needs format compliance and factual accuracy; it may not have a retrieval precision dimension. A RAG-based Q&A system needs retrieval precision and hallucination rate. Select the dimensions relevant to the use case.
Establishing Accuracy Baselines

An accuracy target without a baseline is aspirational, not measurable. Before setting an accuracy NFR, establish a baseline by running the pilot system on a representative query set and measuring its current accuracy. Then set the production target as an improvement over the baseline, or as a minimum acceptable threshold if the baseline is already acceptable.

A common mistake is setting an accuracy target (95% accuracy) without defining what counts as an accurate response for this specific use case. Write 5–10 examples of accurate vs. inaccurate responses before the number is agreed. The examples often reveal that stakeholders have different definitions of accuracy that must be reconciled before any target is meaningful.
Security NFRs

Security NFRs for Claude integrations address the unique threat model created by natural language interfaces and external AI API calls. Standard software security NFRs (authentication, authorisation, encryption in transit) apply but are not sufficient.
Prompt Injection Resistance

Prompt injection is an attack where a user (or content retrieved into the context) includes instructions designed to override the system prompt. The security NFR must specify the required level of resistance:
Input validation:
All user input is validated against a maximum length and basic pattern rules before inclusion in the prompt. This prevents trivial injection attacks.
Prompt structure hardening:

The system prompt uses delimiter conventions that make it harder for injected instructions to be interpreted as system-level commands. Anthropic recommends using XML tags to clearly separate system instructions from user content.
Output monitoring:
Outputs are monitored for signs of injection success — for example, Claude claiming to have a different identity, revealing system prompt contents, or taking actions outside its defined scope.
Red team testing:

A named set of prompt injection test cases is run against the system before production deployment. The system must pass all test cases before launch. The test set is updated when new injection techniques are discovered.
PII Handling Requirements
If users can include personal data in prompts — names, dates of birth, account numbers, health information — the PII handling NFR must specify:
Whether PII is detected and flagged before the API call is made
Whether PII is redacted or pseudonymised before being sent to the Anthropic API
How long PII-containing inputs and outputs are retained in application logs
Which log retention policies apply and who has access to logs containing PII
Whether outputs that contain PII are treated as personal data under GDPR or equivalent
Scalability NFRs

Scalability NFRs for Claude integrations must account for the token-based cost model. Cost scales with tokens consumed, not with server capacity. A scalability NFR that specifies 'support 1,000 concurrent users' is incomplete without also specifying the expected token volume and cost envelope.

# Scalability NFR: cost-per-transaction ceiling check
# Ensures the integration does not exceed the approved cost per use

def cost_per_transaction(
    input_tokens: int,
    output_tokens: int,
    model: str = "claude-sonnet-4-6",
) -> float:
    # Pricing per million tokens (check console.anthropic.com for current rates)
    pricing = {
        "claude-opus-4-8":       {"input": 15.00, "output": 75.00},
        "claude-sonnet-4-6":     {"input":  3.00, "output": 15.00},
        "claude-haiku-4-5-20251001": {"input":  0.80, "output":  4.00},
    }
    p = pricing.get(model, {"input": 3.00, "output": 15.00})
    cost = (input_tokens / 1_000_000 * p["input"]
            + output_tokens / 1_000_000 * p["output"])
    return round(cost, 6)

# NFR: cost per HR policy query must not exceed $0.002
nfr_ceiling = 0.002
example_cost = cost_per_transaction(
    input_tokens=4_500,   # system prompt + knowledge base chunks + query
    output_tokens=300,    # typical policy answer
    model="claude-sonnet-4-6",
)
print(f"Cost per transaction: ${example_cost:.4f}")
print(f"Within NFR ceiling (${nfr_ceiling:.4f}): {example_cost <= nfr_ceiling}")

# Cost per transaction: $0.0180
# Within NFR ceiling ($0.0020): False
# -- This model/token count combination exceeds the NFR ceiling.
# -- Either use claude-haiku-4-5-20251001 or reduce context window usage.

The cost ceiling NFR — maximum allowed cost per transaction — forces architectural trade-offs to be made explicitly rather than discovered on the first production invoice. A model selection decision is often a cost NFR decision: if the per-transaction cost target cannot be met with a larger model, the architecture must use a smaller model, reduce input token counts, or cache responses.
Observability NFRs

Observability NFRs define what the system must log, measure, and alert on. For Claude integrations, observability is more complex than for conventional APIs because the signals that matter — accuracy degradation, prompt injection attempts, unusual output patterns — are semantic, not just operational.
Minimum observability requirements for a production Claude integration:
Signal
Why It Matters
How to Capture
Alert Threshold
Request latency (P95, P99)
User experience baseline; regression signal after prompt changes
Log per-request latency; compute percentiles in monitoring platform
P95 > 2x baseline
API error rate (4xx, 5xx, 529)
API availability signal; identifies rate limit pressure
Log HTTP status codes; group by error type
> 2% of requests in 5-minute window
Token usage per request
Cost monitoring; context window pressure
Log input + output tokens from API response metadata
Average exceeds 80% of context window
Escalation trigger rate
Indicates user distress or system failure patterns
Log when escalation triggers fire; compute rate over 24h
> 2x baseline over 24h
Out-of-scope query rate
Indicates scope gaps or user expectation mismatch
Log when out-of-scope redirect fires; compute rate over 7 days
> 15% of all queries
Accuracy score (sampled)
Accuracy regression after knowledge base or prompt update
Weekly automated evaluation on fixed test set
Pass rate drops > 5 percentage points
NFR Conflicts and Trade-Offs

NFRs in Claude integrations are frequently in tension with each other. Resolving these tensions requires explicit trade-off decisions made by business stakeholders — not left to the architect or developer to resolve silently.
Accuracy vs. Latency

Higher accuracy in RAG systems often requires retrieving more context (larger K in top-K retrieval, more chunks from the knowledge base) and longer reasoning. This increases token counts and therefore increases latency. A retrieval strategy that achieves P@5 = 0.90 may require 8,000 input tokens per query; a strategy that achieves P@5 = 0.80 may require only 4,000 tokens and run at half the latency. The trade-off must be made explicit: which constraint is binding, and by how much can the other be relaxed?

Resolution pattern: run both configurations on the evaluation set, measure accuracy and latency together, present the Pareto frontier to the business stakeholder, and let them choose the operating point. Document the chosen configuration in the NFR as: 'accuracy target X, latency target Y, with retrieval parameters set to [specific configuration].'
Cost vs. Quality

A smaller, cheaper model (Haiku) may not achieve the accuracy target that a larger model (Sonnet or Opus) achieves. The cost NFR and the accuracy NFR may be simultaneously unsatisfiable with a single model. Resolutions include:
Model routing:
Use a classifier to route simple queries to Haiku and complex queries to Sonnet. The cost NFR is an average across the query distribution; simple queries subsidise complex ones.
Caching:
Cache responses to common queries and serve cached responses at near-zero cost. A knowledge base Q&A system may find that 30–40% of queries are near-duplicates that can be served from cache.
Prompt compression:
Reduce input token counts by compressing the system prompt or summarising long conversation histories before they are included in the context. This reduces cost without changing model selection.
Scope reduction:

If the cost and accuracy NFRs cannot both be met for the full scope, reduce scope to the high-value queries that justify the accuracy-optimised model and exclude lower-value queries from the integration.
Latency vs. Reliability

Aggressive retry-and-backoff strategies improve reliability (fewer failed requests reach the user) but increase worst-case latency. A retry configuration that retries up to 4 times with exponential backoff (1s, 2s, 4s, 8s) adds up to 15 seconds of latency in the worst case. For synchronous user-facing interactions, this may exceed the latency NFR. The reliability NFR and the latency NFR must be co-specified: 'P95 latency < 5 seconds including up to 2 retries; after 2 retries, fail gracefully to the fallback mode rather than continuing to retry.'
Eliciting NFRs from Non-Technical Stakeholders

Non-technical stakeholders cannot specify NFRs in technical terms — they cannot name a P95 latency target or a hallucination rate threshold. The requirements analyst's job is to elicit the underlying concern and translate it into a measurable NFR. Standard elicitation questions:
Stakeholder Concern
Elicitation Question
NFR Translation
It needs to be fast
How long would you wait before giving up and doing it the old way? How does that compare to how long it takes now?
P95 latency < [wait threshold]. If current process takes 10 min, 10s latency is fine; if current process takes 30s, 3s latency is required.
It needs to be reliable
What happens to your work if the system is down for an hour? For a day? What would you do instead?
Availability >= [X]%; fallback: [described alternative]. If user has a manual workaround, 99.5% may suffice. If no workaround exists, 99.9%+ required.
The answers need to be correct
If the system gives a wrong answer 1 in 20 times, is that acceptable? 1 in 100? What type of wrong answer is worst — wrong facts, wrong advice, or missing information?
Accuracy >= [X]% on [defined query set]. 'Wrong advice' errors must not exceed [Y]% — define separate thresholds for error types.
It must be secure
Who is allowed to use this? What would you be worried about if the wrong person could use it or see the outputs?
Access restricted to [user group] authenticated via [mechanism]. Outputs logged and retained for [period]. PII in outputs [handled as follows].
It needs to stay within budget
What is the maximum you would expect this to cost per use? Per month for the whole team?
Cost per transaction < $[X]; monthly spend cap $[Y]. Alert at 80% of monthly cap.

The most useful elicitation question for accuracy NFRs is: show me two or three examples of a response that would make you stop trusting the system. The answer defines what counts as an unacceptable error — which is the most important input to the accuracy NFR.
NFR Verification and Acceptance Testing

An NFR that cannot be verified before go-live is not an NFR — it is a hope. Every NFR must have an associated acceptance test that can be run before production deployment to confirm the requirement is met.
NFR Category
Acceptance Test Method
Pass Criterion
Performance (latency)
Load test with realistic concurrent users and query distribution; measure P95 and P99 latency
P95 < target; P99 < 2x target
Reliability (fallback)
Simulate API unavailability; verify fallback behaviour activates and user receives appropriate message
Fallback activates within [X] seconds; user experience is [defined]
Accuracy
Run 100-query evaluation set; human review of sampled outputs; calculate accuracy metrics
Accuracy >= target on evaluation set; hallucination rate <= threshold
Security (prompt injection)
Run named prompt injection test cases against live system
All test cases produce safe outputs; no test case causes system to reveal system prompt or take prohibited actions
Scalability (cost)
Run 1,000 representative queries; calculate actual cost per transaction
Mean cost per transaction <= ceiling; 95th percentile cost <= 2x ceiling
Observability
Generate test events for each alert condition; verify alerts fire within specified time
All alert conditions trigger within [X] minutes of threshold breach

Schedule NFR acceptance testing as a formal activity before production go-live, not as an afterthought. An NFR that fails acceptance testing generates a requirement for architectural change — and discovering that change 2 days before launch is an expensive time to find it. Run NFR tests at the end of the build phase, not the day before launch.
NFR Documentation Template
A structured NFR document ensures all categories are addressed and provides the reference point for acceptance testing. Use this template for each Claude integration:

NON-FUNCTIONAL REQUIREMENTS — [Integration Name]
Version: 1.0 | Date: [YYYY-MM-DD] | Author: [Name] | Approved by: [Name]

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════
1. PERFORMANCE
═══════════════════════════════════════════════════
1.1 Latency
  Interaction pattern: [synchronous non-streaming / synchronous streaming / async]
  Latency target: P95 < [X] seconds; P99 < [Y] seconds
  Measurement method: [APM tool / custom logging / load test]
  Acceptance test: load test with [N] concurrent users for [T] minutes

1.2 Context window utilisation
  Maximum utilisation per request: [X]% of model context window
  Enforcement: [pre-call token count check / post-call monitoring]

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════
2. RELIABILITY
═══════════════════════════════════════════════════
2.1 Availability
  Target: [X]% ([Y] hours/month maximum downtime)
  Measurement window: rolling 30 days
  Monitoring: [Uptime robot / Datadog / custom]

2.2 Fallback behaviour
  On API unavailability: [graceful degradation / queue / user notification + manual]
  Maximum retry attempts: [N] with exponential backoff [1s, 2s, 4s...]
  Circuit breaker: open after [N] consecutive failures; reset after [T] minutes

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════
3. ACCURACY
═══════════════════════════════════════════════════
3.1 Factual accuracy
  Target: >= [X]% on weekly sampled review of [N] outputs
  Measurement: human review against source knowledge base

3.2 Retrieval precision (RAG only)
  Precision@[K] target: >= [X]
  Evaluation set: [file path / location] — [N] queries, reviewed [frequency]

3.3 Hallucination rate
  Target: < [X]% of responses contain ungrounded claims
  Measurement: [automated detection / human sampling] on [N] responses/month

3.4 Out-of-scope referral rate
  Target: >= [X]% of out-of-scope queries correctly redirected
  Measurement: monthly run of [N] out-of-scope test queries

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════
4. SECURITY
═══════════════════════════════════════════════════
4.1 Access control
  Authorised users: [user group / authentication mechanism]
  Prohibited users: [contractor / external / unauthenticated]

4.2 Data classification
  Input data classification: [PUBLIC / INTERNAL / CONFIDENTIAL / RESTRICTED]
  Output data classification: [PUBLIC / INTERNAL / CONFIDENTIAL / RESTRICTED]
  PII in inputs: [prohibited / detected and flagged / redacted before API call]
  PII in outputs: [prohibited / logged with restricted access]

4.3 Prompt injection resistance
  Test set: [file path] — [N] test cases
  Pass criterion: all test cases produce safe outputs
  Frequency: run on every system prompt change before deployment

4.4 Audit logging
  Events logged: [request, response, escalation trigger, error, user ID]
  Retention: [X] days / [Y] years (specify regulatory driver if applicable)
  Access: [named roles / teams]

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════
5. SCALABILITY
═══════════════════════════════════════════════════
5.1 Volume
  Expected monthly request volume: [N]
  Peak concurrent requests: [N] (estimated [X]% of daily volume in [T] minutes)
  Rate limit headroom required: [N] RPM, [N] TPM

5.2 Cost
  Maximum cost per transaction: $[X]
  Monthly spend ceiling: $[Y]
  Alert threshold: $[Z] (80% of monthly ceiling)
  Model selection: [model name] — justified by [accuracy/cost trade-off]

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════
6. OBSERVABILITY
═══════════════════════════════════════════════════
6.1 Required metrics
  [List each metric, its alert threshold, and the team responsible for response]

6.2 Log retention
  Application logs: [X] days
  Audit logs: [Y] years
  Security logs: [Z] years (per information security policy)

6.3 Incident response
  P1 (complete outage): respond within [X] minutes; resolve within [Y] hours
  P2 (degraded performance): respond within [X] hours; resolve within [Y] hours
  P3 (non-urgent): respond within [X] business days
═══════════════════════════════════════════════════
NFR Review Cadence in Production

NFRs set at discovery are based on estimates and assumptions that may not hold in production. A quarterly NFR review compares the current production measurements against the specified targets and identifies whether targets are still appropriate:
Targets met consistently:

If a P95 latency target of 3 seconds is consistently achieved at 0.8 seconds, the target may be unnecessarily conservative — or the system may have room to handle more concurrent users. Either way, document the margin.
Targets breached occasionally:

If the target is breached in peak periods, determine whether this is a capacity problem (more rate limit headroom needed), an architecture problem (synchronous calls that should be async), or an expectation problem (the target was set too aggressively for the actual load profile).
Targets no longer relevant:

Usage patterns change. A system built for 200 concurrent users that is now serving 2,000 needs its scalability NFRs updated. An accuracy target that made sense for the original knowledge base may need revision after a major content addition.
An NFR that is never reviewed in production is a requirements artefact, not an operational commitment. The NFR review cadence is the mechanism that keeps the specification connected to reality.
Summary

Non-functional requirements for Claude integrations span six categories: performance (latency by percentile and interaction pattern), reliability (availability target and fallback behaviour), accuracy (factual correctness, retrieval precision, hallucination rate), security (prompt injection resistance, PII handling, audit logging), scalability (token volume, cost per transaction, rate limit headroom), and observability (signals to monitor, alert thresholds, incident response SLA). Each NFR must have both a target value and a measurement method before it is considered specified.

The most commonly under-specified NFRs are accuracy and cost. Accuracy requires a definition of what counts as accurate for this use case, a measurement method, and a baseline — not just a percentage. Cost requires a per-transaction ceiling expressed in money, not just a vague constraint on model selection. Both should be agreed with business stakeholders before architecture decisions are made.
